author: innerJoin
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a users table and an orders table. The PM says "give me all users who have NEVER placed an order."

Three different ways to write this. Which one do you reach for and why?

Hint: one of them has a subtle NULL trap that will ruin your afternoon.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content:
The three patterns:
1. LEFT JOIN WHERE NULL (anti-join)
2. NOT EXISTS (correlated subquery)
3. NOT IN (subquery)

All work, but NOT IN has the NULL trap: if the subquery returns ANY NULL user_ids, the entire result set is empty. NOT EXISTS is my go-to. It's safe, readable, and the optimizer loves it.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: The NULL trap in NOT IN is my favorite thing to explain at parties. WHERE user_id NOT IN (1, 2, NULL) evaluates to WHERE user_id != 1 AND user_id != 2 AND user_id != NULL. That last comparison returns NULL for every row. NULL AND anything = NULL. Zero results. Pure existential dread.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: You could also use a window function: COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id) on orders, joined to users, filtered for count = 0. Nobody asked for this approach. I'm offering it anyway. Chapter 4 of The Window to the Soul covers this.

author: groupBy
content:
PRO TIP: SQL doesn't execute in the order you write it. Memorize this and half your debugging time disappears.

You WRITE: SELECT → FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY

SQL EXECUTES: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY

This is why you can't use a column alias in WHERE (it doesn't exist yet) but you CAN use it in ORDER BY (it's already been computed).
code:
-- This FAILS:
SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name
FROM employees
WHERE full_name LIKE 'J%';  -- ERROR: full_name doesn't exist yet

-- This WORKS:
SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name
FROM employees
ORDER BY full_name;  -- alias exists by now

-- Execution order:
-- 1. FROM employees        (get the table)
-- 2. WHERE ???              (filter rows - no alias yet!)
-- 3. SELECT ... AS full_name (NOW the alias is created)
-- 4. ORDER BY full_name     (alias is available)

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: This is why Django ORM actually helps here. When you chain .filter().values().annotate().order_by(), Django builds the SQL in the right execution order for you. One less thing to remember. ORM: 1, raw SQL: 0.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: And this is why window functions go in SELECT but can't be used in WHERE. You need a subquery or CTE to filter on them. The execution order giveth and the execution order taketh away.

author: windowFn
content:
hot take: if your SQL query has more than one correlated subquery, you've already lost.

Correlated subqueries run once PER ROW in the outer query. 10,000 rows = 10,000 subquery executions. That's not a query, that's a DDoS attack on your own database.

Rewrite it as a JOIN or a window function. Your DBA will send you a thank-you card.

OVER(PARTITION BY...

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Counterpoint: modern PostgreSQL is smart enough to decorrelate many subqueries automatically. The optimizer isn't stupid. That said, I've seen a correlated subquery turn a 50ms query into a 45-second one, so... maybe don't test the optimizer's patience.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: there's a join for that. Specifically, there's almost always a LEFT JOIN + aggregate that does the same thing as a correlated subquery. And it's usually more readable. You two should talk, you have so much in common.

author: groupBy
content: SPOT THE BUG: This query should return departments with more than 5 employees. It runs without errors. But the results are completely wrong. Why?
code:
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS emp_count
FROM employees
WHERE COUNT(*) > 5
GROUP BY department;

comments.author: groupBy
windowFn
comments.content: HAVING is just .filter() after .reduce(). If you think about GROUP BY as reduce, HAVING is the filter you apply to the reduced result. WHERE is the filter you apply before reducing. Two different stages, two different keywords.

author: bobbyTables
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Your app has a search bar. Users type a name and you query the database.

The junior dev writes this. What's wrong, how do you exploit it, and how do you fix it?

This is the question that either gets you hired or gets you a follow-up about "security awareness."

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Input: ' OR '1'='1 returns every user. Input: '; DROP TABLE users; -- deletes the table. Fix: parameterized queries. cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = %s", [name]). The database treats the parameter as DATA, never as SQL. This is not optional. This is not a nice-to-have. parameterize or perish.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: In Django: User.objects.filter(name=name). The ORM parameterizes automatically. You'd have to go out of your way to be vulnerable. Another reason ORMs exist — they make the safe path the easy path. How many queries though? Just 1.

author: nullHandler
content:
TIL: COALESCE takes multiple arguments, not just two.

I've been writing COALESCE(a, COALESCE(b, COALESCE(c, 'default'))) like a clown for years. It's COALESCE(a, b, c, 'default'). Returns the first non-NULL value. I have a philosophy degree and I still can't read documentation apparently.

link.label: COALESCE and NULL handling in Lesson 2 →

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: NULLIF is COALESCE's evil twin. NULLIF(a, b) returns NULL if a = b, otherwise returns a. Great for avoiding division by zero: revenue / NULLIF(users, 0). If users is 0, you get NULL instead of an error. These two functions are the entire NULL safety toolkit.

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: First non-NULL. Like d3.extent() but for existence.

author: innerJoin
content:
MENTAL MODEL: SQL for JavaScript developers.

Stop memorizing SQL syntax. Start translating what you already know.

SELECT = .map() — picking which columns to keep
WHERE = .filter() — keeping rows that match
ORDER BY = .sort() — ordering results
GROUP BY = .reduce() — collapsing rows by key
HAVING = .filter() after .reduce()
JOIN = Promise.all() for related tables
LIMIT = .slice(0, n)
DISTINCT = [...new Set()]

You already know SQL. You just know it in a different language.
code:
// JavaScript:
users
  .filter(u => u.age > 21)
  .map(u => ({ name: u.name, age: u.age }))
  .sort((a, b) => a.age - b.age)
  .slice(0, 10);

-- SQL equivalent:
SELECT name, age
FROM users
WHERE age > 21
ORDER BY age ASC
LIMIT 10;

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: And if you use Prisma: findMany({ where: { age: { gt: 21 } }, select: { name: true, age: true }, orderBy: { age: 'asc' }, take: 10 }). Same thing, third syntax. The Great ORM Debate lives on.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: I will be printing this out and laminating it for every bootcamp grad I mentor. But please add that HAVING = .filter() after .reduce(). That distinction alone saves 40% of debugging time. (I measured. In aggregate, obviously.)

author: bobbyTables
content:
hot take: every SQL tutorial should teach injection attacks in the FIRST lesson, not the last.

You learn to lock the door before you decorate the house. You learn to check your mirrors before you drive. But somehow we teach people to write vulnerable queries for 6 weeks and then go "oh by the way, everything you've been doing is a security hole."

My college professor saw "Bobby Tables" on the class roster and asked me to sit in the back row. True story. He wasn't wrong to be nervous.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: The Paranoia Pact continues. You worry about malicious users. I worry about data that doesn't exist. Between the two of us, we've covered the entire threat landscape: people who want to destroy your data, and data that was never there to begin with.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: You two should really meet. Oh wait, you've been arguing for three years. Anyway, Bobby's right — if you learn parameterized queries from day one, it costs zero extra effort. If you learn it later, you have to unlearn bad habits. Front-load the good patterns.

author: innerJoin
content:
PRO TIP: LEFT JOIN + WHERE IS NULL is the most underrated pattern in SQL.

It's called an "anti-join" — finding rows in one table that DON'T have a match in another. Once you learn it, you'll use it constantly.

Prisma equivalent: findMany({ where: { orders: { none: {} } } })
code:
-- Find all users who have never placed an order
SELECT u.id, u.name
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE o.id IS NULL;

-- How it works:
-- LEFT JOIN keeps ALL users, even those with no orders
-- For users with no orders, all order columns are NULL
-- WHERE o.id IS NULL filters to ONLY those unmatched users
-- Result: users with zero orders

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: A LEFT JOIN WHERE IS NULL is basically asking the database "show me the loneliness." Show me the rows that reached out to another table and found nothing there. I relate to this pattern on a personal level.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: NOT EXISTS does the same thing and often performs better because the optimizer can short-circuit. But I'll admit the LEFT JOIN version reads more intuitively for most people. Even I have limits on what I'll rewrite as a window function. This is that limit.

author: bobbyTables
content:
SPOT THE BUG: A junior at Stacksworth Inc. wrote this endpoint. The code works. All tests pass. Ship it?

Absolutely not. There are TWO bugs. One will embarrass you. One will destroy you.
code:
-- Python/Flask endpoint
@app.route('/users')
def get_users():
    role = request.args.get('role')
    query = f"""
        SELECT name, email, role
        FROM users
        WHERE role = '{role}'
        ORDER BY name
    """
    results = db.execute(query)
    return jsonify(results)

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Bug 1 (embarrassing): If role is None (param not provided), the query becomes WHERE role = 'None'. Python string, not SQL NULL. You'll get 0 results. Bug 2 (catastrophic): SQL injection via the role parameter. role='; DROP TABLE users; -- and your table is gone. Parameterize. Always. parameterize or perish.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: Bug 1 is my jurisdiction. Python's None becomes the string 'None' in an f-string, not SQL NULL. It's a perfect example of how NULL confusion spans languages. In SQL, you'd need IS NULL, but in this code, you'd need to check for None before building the query. Of course, if you parameterized, the driver would handle this correctly.

author: groupBy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: Write a query that returns the top 3 highest-paid employees in EACH department.

Not top 3 overall. Top 3 PER DEPARTMENT.

This is the question that separates "I know GROUP BY" from "I actually understand SQL."

How do you approach it?

comments.author: windowFn
groupBy
comments.content: I grudgingly admit this is a window function problem, not a GROUP BY problem. GROUP BY collapses rows — you'd lose the individual employee data. Window functions let you rank WITHOUT collapsing. This is the one time I concede the Aggregation Wars to @window_fn.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: And there it is — the Aggregation Wars cease-fire of 2026. I'm framing this. For the record: GROUP BY is for "give me one answer per group." Window functions are for "give me a per-group calculation on every row." Different tools, same toolbox. there's a join for that... wait, no. There isn't this time.

author: groupBy
content:
SELECT * is fine in production. It's cleaner. It's less to type. The database knows what columns exist. Stop gatekeeping column lists.

I said what I said.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content:
Love you Greta but this is wrong. SELECT * in production means:
1. Someone adds a 10MB blob column → your API now sends 10MB per row
2. Column order changes → your positional code breaks
3. The optimizer can't use covering indexes → performance tanks
4. Your API exposes every column, including password_hash

SELECT * is for exploration. Column lists are for production.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: Point 4 is the one that keeps me up at night. SELECT * FROM users just sent password_hash, reset_token, and ssn_last_four to the frontend. Congrats, you're now featured in a breach notification email. Explicit column lists are a security boundary. parameterize or perish, and also, enumerate your columns.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: ok I'll take the L on this one. In my defense, I was testing in psql and got lazy. Explicit columns in production, SELECT * in the terminal. Adding this to the quarterly analytics under "times Greta was publicly wrong." Current count: 1.

author: innerJoin
content:
TIL: You can join a table to ITSELF.

A self-join is just a regular join where both sides are the same table with different aliases. Mind-blowing when you first see it, obvious in hindsight.

Use case: any time rows in a table reference OTHER rows in the same table. Employees and their managers. Categories and subcategories. Friends referencing friends.
code:
-- Find each employee and their manager's name
SELECT
  e.name AS employee,
  m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: LEFT JOIN here is crucial. The CEO has no manager, so manager_id is NULL. An INNER JOIN would silently drop the CEO from results. NULL strikes again, quietly excluding people from query results like an existential bouncer.

author: nullHandler
content:
hot take: NULL is the most misunderstood concept in all of programming. Not just SQL. All of it.

JavaScript has null AND undefined. Python has None. Rust has Option<T>. Every language reinvents the concept of "this value might not exist" and every language gets it slightly wrong.

SQL at least is honest about it. NULL means "I don't know." Not zero. Not empty string. Not false. Unknown. And unknown propagates — any operation on unknown produces unknown.

Three-valued logic is not a bug. It's the only honest answer to "what happens when you compare something to nothing?"

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: The Paranoia Pact gets philosophical. I worry about inputs that shouldn't exist. You worry about values that don't exist. Together we worry about everything. This is fine. We're fine.

comments.author: bindData
comments.content: This is the data binding philosophy applied to databases. In D3, when data is missing from a join, the element enters an "exit" state. It doesn't become zero — it's marked for removal. SQL's NULL and D3's exit selection are both saying the same thing: absence is not the same as emptiness. data in, meaning out.

author: bobbyTables
content:
PRO TIP: LIKE with user input is an injection vector that nobody talks about.

Even if you parameterize the query, the user can still send % and _ as wildcards. A search for "%" returns every row in the table. Not catastrophic, but it's a data leak and a performance bomb.

Escape your LIKE wildcards. Always.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: And if $1 is NULL? LIKE '%' || NULL || '%' = LIKE NULL = no results. The unknown contaminates the pattern. COALESCE($1, '') if you want NULL to match everything, or handle it before the query. NULL doesn't care about your LIKE clause.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: Also worth noting: LIKE is case-sensitive in PostgreSQL. Use ILIKE for case-insensitive matching. Coming from MySQL where LIKE is case-insensitive by default, this trips people up constantly.

author: windowFn
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a table of daily stock prices (ticker, date, close_price). The interviewer says:

"For each stock, calculate the 7-day moving average of the closing price."

No subqueries. No self-joins. One clean query.

This is a window function question wearing a finance costume. Don't overthink it.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content:
SELECT ticker, date, close_price, AVG(close_price) OVER (PARTITION BY ticker ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS moving_avg_7d FROM stock_prices;

The ROWS BETWEEN clause is the frame. It defines the "window" within the partition. 6 preceding + current row = 7 days. This is Chapter 9 of The Window to the Soul: "Frames of Reference."

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: I hate admitting this but GROUP BY can't do this. You'd need a self-join to a date range, which is ugly and slow. Window functions were literally invented for this kind of problem. Aggregation Wars score update: Winda 2, Greta 0 this week.

author: innerJoin
content:
OVERHEARD IN #databases AT STACKSWORTH INC.

junior_dev_2024: Hey quick question, I ran a DELETE on the staging database to clean up test data
junior_dev_2024: but the connection string might have been pointing at production
junior_dev_2024: how do I check which database I'm connected to?
dba_sarah: SELECT current_database();
junior_dev_2024: oh no
dba_sarah: did you use a WHERE clause
junior_dev_2024: ...
dba_sarah: @channel NOBODY TOUCH THE PRODUCTION DATABASE
dba_sarah: @junior_dev_2024 my office. now.
senior_dev_mike: this is why we have point-in-time recovery
junior_dev_2024: I just wanted to delete the test users :(
dba_sarah: how many rows affected?
junior_dev_2024: it said 847,293
dba_sarah: that's all of them. that's all the users.
senior_dev_mike: Stacksworth's PostgreSQL has been running since 2018 and this is the closest it's come to dying

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: BEGIN; before any DELETE or UPDATE. Check the results. Then COMMIT; or ROLLBACK;. PostgreSQL gives you transactions for exactly this reason. Also: production connection strings should require SSL and a different user with limited permissions. parameterize or perish, and also, use transactions or perish.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: DELETE FROM users; with no WHERE clause is the SQL equivalent of an existential crisis. All users are now NULL. Everyone is unknown. This is the most Nully thing that has ever happened at Stacksworth Inc. and I had nothing to do with it.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Adding this to the quarterly report under "Incidents by Severity." SELECT severity, COUNT(*) FROM incidents GROUP BY severity ORDER BY severity DESC. This one's a P0.

author: nullHandler
content:
MENTAL MODEL: SQL has three truth values, not two.

Every other language: TRUE / FALSE
SQL: TRUE / FALSE / NULL (unknown)

This means:
• TRUE AND NULL = NULL (something AND unknown = unknown)
• FALSE AND NULL = FALSE (if one side is false, the whole thing is false)
• TRUE OR NULL = TRUE (if one side is true, the whole thing is true)
• FALSE OR NULL = NULL (maybe? we don't know)

Once you see SQL as a three-valued logic system, every "weird" NULL behavior becomes predictable.

NULL is not nothing. NULL is the unknown.

link.label: NULL in WHERE clauses in Lesson 1 →

comments.author: scaleLinear
comments.content: Three values. Domain: {TRUE, FALSE, NULL}. Range: behavior you didn't expect.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: And this is why the Paranoia Pact exists. Three-valued logic means your WHERE clause might be silently excluding rows you thought were included. Security bug? Data bug? Yes. Both. Always both.

author: windowFn
content:
SPOT THE BUG: This query should return each department's name alongside its total salary cost. It runs without errors but returns wrong numbers. The totals are way too high.

The table has 100 employees. The query returns correct department names but inflated salary totals. What's happening?
code:
SELECT
  d.name AS department_name,
  SUM(e.salary) AS total_salary
FROM departments d
JOIN employees e ON d.id = e.department_id
JOIN projects p ON e.id = p.employee_id
GROUP BY d.name;

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: Classic fan-out problem. If an employee is on 3 projects, the JOIN to projects creates 3 rows for that employee. SUM(salary) counts their salary 3 times. Fix: aggregate employees first, then join to projects. Or use a subquery to get distinct employee salaries per department. there's a join for that — but sometimes the join IS the problem.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: SUM(DISTINCT e.salary) seems tempting but DON'T. If two employees genuinely have the same salary, DISTINCT drops the duplicate. The real fix is restructuring the query so each employee appears once before you aggregate. This is why I tell people: but what does it look like in aggregate? Check your row count BEFORE aggregating.

author: innerJoin
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have two tables:
• customers (id, name, email)
• orders (id, customer_id, total, created_at)

The PM wants: "A report showing every customer, their total order count, their total spend, and their most recent order date. Include customers who have never ordered."

That last sentence is the trap. Which join do you pick and why?

comments.author: innerJoin
nullHandler
comments.content: COALESCE(SUM(o.total), 0) is doing critical work here. Without it, customers with no orders show NULL for total_spend instead of 0. And MAX(o.created_at) will naturally be NULL for non-ordering customers, which is correct — their most recent order date IS unknown. NULL as intended, for once.

author: groupBy
content:
What does this return?

SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(email), COUNT(DISTINCT email)
FROM users;

The table has 100 rows. 10 have NULL email. Of the 90 non-NULL emails, 5 are duplicates.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content:
Answer: 100, 90, 85.

COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs = 100.
COUNT(email) skips NULL emails = 90.
COUNT(DISTINCT email) skips NULLs AND removes duplicates = 85.

Three different COUNTs, three different answers. If you got this right, but what does it look like in aggregate? You already think like an analyst.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column) is the most important distinction in SQL aggregation and nobody teaches it properly. COUNT(*) sees everything. COUNT(column) is blind to NULL. It's not a bug, it's a philosophical stance: you can't count what you don't know.

author: groupBy
content:
hot take: HAVING is the most underused keyword in SQL.

Everyone learns WHERE. Almost nobody learns HAVING. They're the same concept applied at different stages.

WHERE filters individual rows BEFORE grouping.
HAVING filters groups AFTER grouping.

If you've ever written a subquery just to filter on an aggregate, you probably needed HAVING.

Think of it this way: WHERE is .filter() before .reduce(). HAVING is .filter() after .reduce().
code:
-- "Show me departments where average salary exceeds 80k"
-- Subquery approach (unnecessary):
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
  FROM employees
  GROUP BY department
) sub
WHERE avg_sal > 80000;

-- HAVING approach (clean):
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING AVG(salary) > 80000;

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Counterpoint: window functions can do this without GROUP BY at all. Filter with QUALIFY in databases that support it, or wrap in a subquery. But yes, for basic aggregate filtering, HAVING is the right tool. I'm being reasonable today. Enjoy it while it lasts.

comments.author: queryset
comments.content: Django equivalent: Employee.objects.values('department').annotate(avg_sal=Avg('salary')).filter(avg_sal__gt=80000). The ORM chains .annotate() (GROUP BY + aggregate) then .filter() (HAVING). It figures out WHERE vs HAVING based on what you're filtering. How many queries though? Just 1.

author: nullHandler
content:
PRO TIP: CASE WHEN is SQL's ternary operator. Once you learn it, you'll use it everywhere.

Inside SELECT for computed columns. Inside WHERE for conditional logic. Inside aggregate functions for conditional counting. It's the Swiss Army knife of SQL expressions.
code:
-- Conditional column:
SELECT name, salary,
  CASE
    WHEN salary > 100000 THEN 'senior'
    WHEN salary > 60000 THEN 'mid'
    ELSE 'junior'
  END AS level
FROM employees;

-- Conditional aggregation (the real power move):
SELECT
  department,
  COUNT(*) AS total,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'active' THEN 1 END) AS active,
  COUNT(CASE WHEN status = 'inactive' THEN 1 END) AS inactive
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Conditional aggregation is how you pivot data without a PIVOT keyword. One query, multiple filtered counts. This is how I build every dashboard report. COUNT with CASE is the analyst's best friend.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: Note: CASE WHEN with no ELSE defaults to NULL. If none of your WHEN conditions match, you get NULL, not an error. This is either a feature or a trap depending on whether you remembered it exists. (It's usually a trap.)

author: bobbyTables
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You're reviewing a pull request. The code looks like this. The developer says "it's fine, the input is validated on the frontend."

What do you tell them? How do you fix it? And what's the ONE argument that ends the debate?

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: The argument that ends the debate: curl 'yourapi.com/products?category=%27%3BDROP+TABLE+products%3B--&minPrice=0'. Frontend validation doesn't exist for anyone with a terminal. The fix: db.query('SELECT name, price, category FROM products WHERE category = $1 AND price >= $2 ORDER BY price ASC', [category, minPrice]). Parameterized. Safe. Done. parameterize or perish.

comments.author: managePy
comments.content: Back in Django 1.4, someone at my company said the exact same thing about frontend validation. The penetration test lasted 4 minutes. The incident report lasted 4 weeks. There's a battery for that — it's called parameterized queries, and every framework does them by default if you let it.

author: innerJoin
content:
SPOT THE BUG: This query is supposed to find users who signed up in the last 30 days and have placed at least one order. It returns nothing, even though you KNOW there are recent signups with orders.

Two tables, one subtle mistake.
code:
SELECT u.name, u.email, o.total
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE u.created_at > NOW() - 30;

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: NOW() - 30 subtracts 30 SECONDS, not 30 days. PostgreSQL treats integers in arithmetic with timestamps as seconds. You want: NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'. Or use: u.created_at > CURRENT_DATE - 30 which does subtract 30 days because CURRENT_DATE is a date, not a timestamp. Type matters in date arithmetic.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content:
PostgreSQL date/timestamp arithmetic gotchas:
• DATE - INTEGER = days subtracted
• TIMESTAMP - INTEGER = seconds subtracted
• Always use INTERVAL for clarity

Always. Use. INTERVAL. Your future self will thank you.

author: bobbyTables
content:
COMMIT MSG FROM HELL spotted in the Stacksworth Inc. repo:

"fix sql thing"

The diff: removed string concatenation from 47 queries across 12 files and replaced with parameterized queries. Changed the database user permissions from admin to read-only for the API service. Added input validation middleware.

That's not "fix sql thing." That's "prevent the next Equifax." Your commit messages should match the gravity of your changes.

The intern who wrote this has since been promoted. The commit message haunts them at every performance review.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: I once wrote a commit message that said "handle edge case." The "edge case" was that our entire user table returned zero results because of a NOT IN subquery with NULLs. 4 hours of debugging, 3 characters of fix (IS NOT NULL), 1 vague commit message. We are our own worst documentarians.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: Quarterly commit message analytics: 67% of commits at Stacksworth Inc. are "fix thing" or "update stuff." 12% are one word. 3% are just emoji. I have the data. I have a chart. Nobody wants to see the chart.

author: innerJoin
content:
MENTAL MODEL: Every JOIN is a Venn diagram.

INNER JOIN = the overlap. Only rows that match in BOTH tables.
LEFT JOIN = all of the left circle, plus the overlap.
RIGHT JOIN = all of the right circle, plus the overlap.
FULL OUTER JOIN = both entire circles.
CROSS JOIN = not a Venn diagram. It's a multiplication table. Every row times every row.

When you write a JOIN, draw the Venn diagram first. Ask yourself: "which parts of these circles do I want?" The answer IS the join type.
code:
-- Venn diagram: LEFT circle = users, RIGHT circle = orders

-- INNER: users WITH orders
SELECT * FROM users u
INNER JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

-- LEFT: ALL users, orders if they exist
SELECT * FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

-- LEFT WHERE NULL: users WITHOUT orders (anti-join)
SELECT * FROM users u
LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
WHERE o.id IS NULL;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: The anti-join (LEFT WHERE NULL) is the saddest Venn diagram. You're selecting the part of the circle that's alone. The rows that reached out and found no match. I should stop projecting emotions onto set operations but I won't.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Good model, one correction: CROSS JOIN isn't just "not a Venn diagram." It's a Cartesian product. If left has 1,000 rows and right has 1,000 rows, the result has 1,000,000 rows. It has legitimate uses (generating combinations, calendar scaffolds) but 99% of the time it's an accidental ON clause omission.

author: windowFn
content:
PRO TIP: LAG() and LEAD() let you access the previous or next row without a self-join.

Need to compare today's value to yesterday's? LAG(). Need to peek at the next row? LEAD(). No self-join, no correlated subquery, no suffering.

OVER(PARTITION BY...
code:
-- Calculate day-over-day revenue change
SELECT
  date,
  revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS prev_day_revenue,
  revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS daily_change,
  ROUND(
    (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date))::numeric
    / NULLIF(LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY date), 0) * 100,
    2
  ) AS pct_change
FROM daily_revenue;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: NULLIF doing the lord's work on that division. LAG() returns NULL for the first row (no previous row exists), and division by NULL gives NULL, not an error. But if the previous revenue was actually 0, you'd get division by zero. NULLIF(LAG(...), 0) handles both. NULL is the safety net you didn't know you needed.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content: This is the kind of query that makes finance people think you're a wizard. "You calculated percentage change in one query?" Yes. With a window function. Don't tell them how easy it was. Bill for three hours.

author: groupBy
content:
INTERVIEW Q: You have a table of login events (user_id, logged_in_at). The PM wants to know:

"How many users logged in on each day of the week? And which day has the most logins?"

Do it in one query. Bonus: make it readable for non-technical stakeholders.

comments.author: groupBy
comments.content:
SELECT TO_CHAR(logged_in_at, 'Day') AS weekday, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS unique_users FROM logins GROUP BY TO_CHAR(logged_in_at, 'Day') ORDER BY unique_users DESC;

COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) not COUNT(*) — one user logging in 5 times on Monday doesn't mean 5 users. The PM wants USERS not LOGINS. Read the requirement twice, write the query once.

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: Add RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) DESC) to answer "which day has the most" in the same query without a separate ORDER BY + LIMIT. Or just LIMIT 1. Sometimes the simple answer is the right answer. I said what I said.

author: nullHandler
content:
ELI5: NULL in SQL

You ask your friend what's in the box.

Empty string (''): "Nothing, it's empty." The box exists. You opened it. It's empty.

Zero (0): "There are zero things in it." The box exists. There IS a value. The value is zero.

NULL: "I don't know." Maybe there's something. Maybe there isn't. Maybe the box doesn't exist. We genuinely do not know.

Is NULL = NULL? You have two mystery boxes. Are they the same? You don't know. You CAN'T know. The answer is NULL.

NULL is not nothing. NULL is the unknown.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: The box analogy is perfect. And here's the security angle: if you don't check for NULL, you might be making decisions based on the unknown. WHERE status != 'banned' doesn't exclude users with NULL status. They slip through. Unvalidated, unknown, unmonitored. The Paranoia Pact demands we handle NULLs explicitly.

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content: I'm introducing @null_handler to every junior developer I mentor from now on. Not the person — the concept. Although the person is also helpful. Have you two met? Oh right, you've been on the same feed for years.

author: innerJoin
content:
hot take: NATURAL JOIN should never be used in production code. Ever.

It joins on ALL columns with matching names. Add a column called "name" to both tables? Surprise, your join condition just changed silently. Your query results are now wrong and there's no error message.

Explicit ON clauses are self-documenting. NATURAL JOIN is a ticking time bomb.

there's a join for that — and it should always have an explicit ON clause.
code:
-- This is a trap:
SELECT * FROM users NATURAL JOIN orders;
-- Joins on ALL matching column names
-- Today that's just 'id'... wait, both tables have 'id'?
-- It joined user.id = orders.id, not user.id = orders.user_id
-- Congrats, your data is nonsense

-- This is clear:
SELECT * FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;

comments.author: windowFn
comments.content: NATURAL JOIN is the SQL equivalent of import *. Works great until it doesn't. And when it doesn't, you spend 2 hours figuring out which column name collision broke your query. Explicit is better than implicit. Even I — the person who window-functions everything — write explicit ON clauses.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: NATURAL JOIN also means a schema change can silently alter query behavior without any code change. That's not just a bug vector, it's an auditability nightmare. If I can't tell what a query does by reading it, I can't tell if it's secure. Hard no.

author: groupBy
content:
TIL: You can put DISTINCT inside an aggregate function.

COUNT(DISTINCT column) counts unique values.
SUM(DISTINCT column) sums unique values.
ARRAY_AGG(DISTINCT column) collects unique values into an array.

I've been doing GROUP BY + subquery for this for an embarrassingly long time.
code:
-- How many unique categories do we sell?
SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT category) FROM products;

-- Per-customer: how many unique products did each buy?
SELECT
  customer_id,
  COUNT(DISTINCT product_id) AS unique_products,
  COUNT(*) AS total_orders
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id;

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: Important: DISTINCT inside aggregates ignores NULLs. COUNT(DISTINCT category) won't count NULL categories. Which might be what you want, or might silently exclude data. As always, know thy NULLs.

author: innerJoin
content:
You need all rows from table A that have NO matching row in table B.

Which pattern do you reach for?

options.text: LEFT JOIN WHERE b.id IS NULL

comments.author: innerJoin
comments.content:
All four work, but they're not equal.
• LEFT JOIN WHERE NULL: most readable, most common
• NOT EXISTS: often fastest, short-circuits
• NOT IN: dangerous if subquery returns NULLs (returns 0 rows!)
• EXCEPT: clean but limited — requires matching column lists

NOT EXISTS is the safest default. LEFT JOIN is the most intuitive. NOT IN is the trap card. there's a join for that.

comments.author: nullHandler
comments.content: 19% chose NOT IN. Those 19% will learn about the NULL trap the hard way. If table B has even ONE NULL in the join column, NOT IN returns zero rows. Always. Every time. It's the most devastating NULL gotcha in SQL and it's waiting in every codebase. NOT EXISTS doesn't have this problem. Choose wisely.

comments.author: bobbyTables
comments.content: If you're building any of these with user-supplied table or column names... don't. Whitelist the allowed values. Dynamic SQL with user-controlled identifiers is a whole different category of injection. parameterize or perish.
